run.output:
Q1 result count: 4 rows
Execution time: 728.65 ms

validation.comparison_result: Q1 results match ground truth (4 rows expected, 4 rows actual)

Q1.notes: Correctness validated. Performance improved 5.6% vs iter_1 (771.68ms → 728.65ms) with MADV_WILLNEED, reduced morsel size (16K), and 2x loop unrolling. Still 15.2x slower than 48ms baseline and 8.4x slower than DuckDB (87ms).

Q1.bottleneck: CPU-bound: Inefficient SIMD mask extraction pattern with scattered memory access across 6 columns for each matching row
Q1.root_cause: Current SIMD implementation (lines 270-344) uses bitmask iteration (_mm512_cmp_epi32_mask → __builtin_ctz → load other columns for each match). With 98.8% selectivity, almost every row passes the filter, making the mask extraction overhead (2-3 instructions per row: ctz, mask clear, index compute) and scattered column loads (6 separate memory accesses per row) the primary bottleneck. For 60M rows × 6 columns × ~5 cycles per random access = ~1.8B cycles = ~450ms at 4GHz, explaining most of the runtime. Better approach: process rows sequentially with SIMD for arithmetic (no mask extraction), or prefetch all columns for the morsel upfront and use SOA layout for better spatial locality.

analysis.overall: Q1 iteration 2 shows incremental progress (5.6% speedup vs iter_1) from MADV_WILLNEED, smaller morsel size (50K→16K for L1 cache), and 2x loop unrolling. However, the fundamental bottleneck remains: inefficient SIMD filter pattern for high-selectivity predicates. At 98.8% selectivity, the date filter eliminates only 1.2% of rows, so the SIMD compare + bitmask extraction overhead exceeds any benefit. The scattered memory access pattern across 6 columns (returnflag, linestatus, quantity, price, discount, tax) for each matching row creates poor cache locality. Hardware: 64 cores, AVX-512, 376GB RAM, 44MB L3, HDD (but data is in page cache given the ~730ms runtime vs theoretical 11s I/O time for 1.7GB at 150MB/s). The 48ms baseline likely uses a simpler, more cache-friendly approach: sequential scan without SIMD mask extraction, or better prefetching, or streaming aggregation.

performance_optimizations.technique: Eliminate SIMD Mask Extraction for High-Selectivity Filters
performance_optimizations.description: At 98.8% selectivity, the SIMD date filter is counterproductive. Replace the current mask-extraction pattern (lines 270-344) with one of two approaches: (A) Sequential scalar code with branch prediction: `if (shipdate <= cutoff) { /* aggregate */ }` — branch predictor will correctly predict ~99% of branches, eliminating misprediction penalty. (B) Keep SIMD for date comparison but process ALL rows (not just matches) with branchless aggregation: use SIMD mask to conditionally accumulate (_mm512_mask_add_epi32). Approach (A) is simpler and likely faster given high selectivity and modern CPU branch prediction (>95% accuracy).
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 2-3x speedup (728ms → 240-365ms). Eliminates bitmask extraction overhead (ctz, mask clearing), scattered column loads become sequential, and branch predictor keeps pipeline full. This is likely how the 48ms baseline works.

performance_optimizations.technique: Remove MADV_WILLNEED on HDD with Large Dataset
performance_optimizations.description: The code now uses both MADV_SEQUENTIAL and MADV_WILLNEED (lines 138-139). With 7 columns × 60M rows × 26 bytes/row = ~1.7GB total and all columns mmap'd simultaneously, MADV_WILLNEED triggers immediate prefetch of all 1.7GB. On HDD, this causes all 7 column files to compete for disk bandwidth simultaneously, potentially causing seek storms. While iter_2 showed improvement with WILLNEED (likely due to warm cache), removing it and relying solely on MADV_SEQUENTIAL lets the kernel schedule I/O more efficiently. Test both: current (SEQUENTIAL+WILLNEED) may be optimal for SSD or warm cache; SEQUENTIAL-only may be better for cold HDD.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 10-20% speedup on cold cache (730ms → 585-655ms) or neutral on warm cache. Combined with priority 1: 240-365ms → 190-290ms.

performance_optimizations.description: Current code accesses 6 columns (returnflag, linestatus, quantity, price, discount, tax) for each matching row index (lines 288-296, 321-329, 359-367). These accesses are unpredictable to the hardware prefetcher due to sparse matching pattern (even at 98.8% selectivity, the exact positions vary). Add explicit software prefetching: for each match at index idx, prefetch the next likely match at idx+1, idx+2, etc.: `_mm_prefetch(&l_quantity.data[idx+4], _MM_HINT_T0)` for all 6 columns. Prefetch distance of 4-8 rows ahead hides memory latency (~200 cycles DRAM) behind computation (~50 cycles per row).
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 15-25% speedup (730ms → 550-620ms). Reduces memory stalls by overlapping data fetch with computation. Most effective if combined with priority 1 (sequential scan), as sequential pattern makes prefetching more predictable.

performance_optimizations.description: Code uses Kahan summation for sum_disc_price and sum_charge (lines 82-95, called at 303-304, 336-337, 374-375). Kahan summation adds 5 FP operations per accumulation to maintain precision. For 60M rows × 2 calls × 5 ops = 600M extra FP ops. At scale factor 10, standard double precision (53-bit mantissa) is sufficient for TPC-H accuracy requirements. Replace Kahan summation with simple += operator: change add_disc_price(val) to sum_disc_price += val. Verify results still match ground truth (compare_results.py should pass with default tolerance). If validation fails, use pairwise summation (hierarchical reduction) instead of Kahan — better accuracy than naive with less overhead.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 5-10% speedup (730ms → 655-695ms). Small per-op savings multiply across 60M rows. Risk: may cause validation failure if ground truth expects high precision. Test carefully.

performance_optimizations.technique: Reduce Function Call Overhead in Hot Path
performance_optimizations.description: The aggregation update calls add_disc_price() and add_charge() (lines 303-304, 336-337, 374-375) which are inline functions with 6 FP operations each. Even inlined, the Kahan summation creates register pressure and prevents compiler optimizations. If keeping Kahan (not recommended per priority 4), manually inline these calls and use explicit register allocation hints (__attribute__((always_inline))). Better: remove Kahan entirely per priority 4.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 3-5% speedup if Kahan is kept (730ms → 695-710ms). Zero benefit if Kahan is removed (covered by priority 4).

performance_optimizations.description: Reduced morsel size from 50K to 16K (line 239) improves L1 cache hit rate but increases morsel boundary overhead: more atomic range_idx fetches (line 257), more loop iterations. With 60M rows / 16K = 3750 morsels and 64 threads, each thread processes ~59 morsels, fetching range_idx 59 times. At 16K morsel size, each morsel processes in ~3-4ms (730ms / 64 threads / 3.6 morsels ≈ 3.2ms), so atomic overhead (~50-100 cycles per fetch = 12-25ns) is negligible (<1% of runtime). However, smaller morsels mean more L2/L3 cache pressure from switching contexts. Test morsel sizes: 8K (fits L1 tighter), 16K (current), 32K (reduces boundary overhead). Profile L1 cache miss rate to determine optimal size.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 2-5% speedup with optimal morsel size (730ms → 695-715ms). Requires profiling to find sweet spot between cache hit rate and overhead.

performance_optimizations.technique: Column File Interleaving for Better Spatial Locality
performance_optimizations.description: Current storage design (storage_design.json) uses separate binary files per column: l_shipdate.bin, l_quantity.bin, etc. When processing a row, we access 7 separate files, causing poor spatial locality on HDD (7 separate read streams). Alternative: use row-oriented blocks or hybrid PAX layout (page-at-a-time): within each 100K-row block, store all columns contiguously. This requires storage format changes (not query code changes), so out of scope for current iteration. Note: columnar storage is optimal for column pruning (Q1 uses 7/16 columns) but PAX layout provides better within-block locality.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 15-30% speedup on cold cache HDD (730ms → 510-620ms first run). Minimal impact on warm cache. Requires storage format redesign.

performance_optimizations.technique: Use Wider SIMD for Aggregation (AVX-512 Horizontal Ops)
performance_optimizations.description: Current aggregation is scalar after SIMD filtering (lines 301-309). Since quantities, prices, and discounts are all integer types, use SIMD horizontal aggregation: accumulate 16 values at a time in vector registers (_mm512_add_epi32 for qty, _mm512_add_epi64 for price), reduce to scalar only at morsel end. Challenge: perfect hash (returnflag × linestatus) creates 6 buckets, hard to vectorize hash table updates. Alternative: accumulate totals without grouping first (SIMD), then split by groups at end. Won't work for GROUP BY but Q1 has only 4 groups, so overhead is minimal. More viable: keep scalar aggregation (it's not the bottleneck per root cause analysis).
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 5-10% speedup if implemented correctly (730ms → 655-695ms). High implementation complexity for uncertain gain. Priority 1 (sequential scan) is more impactful.

performance_optimizations.technique: Branch-Free Date Comparison for Unpredictable Cases
performance_optimizations.description: If date filter selectivity were ~50% (unpredictable branches), branch-free filtering would help: `int passes = (shipdate <= cutoff); agg[passes] += value;` uses arithmetic instead of branches. However, Q1 has 98.8% selectivity (highly predictable), so branch prediction works well. Branch-free filtering would actually HURT performance here by always executing the aggregation path even for non-matching rows. Only use branch-free filtering when selectivity is 20-80%.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: Negative impact at 98.8% selectivity (730ms → 800-900ms). Not recommended for Q1. Useful technique for Q6 (2.4% selectivity with multiple filters → ~30% compound selectivity).

summary: Q1 iteration 2 shows incremental progress (5.6% speedup vs iter_1: 771.68ms → 728.65ms) from MADV_WILLNEED prefetching, smaller morsel size (16K for L1 cache), and 2x loop unrolling. Results are correct. However, we're still 15.2x slower than the 48ms baseline and 8.4x slower than DuckDB (87ms). Root cause: SIMD mask extraction overhead + scattered memory access across 6 columns. At 98.8% selectivity, the date filter eliminates only 720K rows out of 60M, making the SIMD bitmask iteration (ctz, mask clearing) and sparse column loads counterproductive. Recommendation: Priority 1 (eliminate SIMD mask extraction → sequential scan with branch prediction) provides 2-3x speedup (→ 240-365ms), bringing us within 3-8x of baseline. Combine with priorities 2-4 (remove MADV_WILLNEED on cold HDD, add software prefetching, optimize Kahan summation) to reach ~190-290ms (4-6x from baseline, competitive with DuckDB). The 48ms baseline likely uses a cache-oblivious sequential scan optimized for high-selectivity filters, avoiding SIMD overhead entirely.
